Justin Joly.lateround_profile: Justin Joly is a converted wide receiver with really strong age-adjusted production numbers (including an 88th-percentile Breakout Score among the tight end database). He was a borderline top-100 pick at various points in the pre-draft process but was finally taken off the board 152nd overall by the Denver Broncos. While the draft capital was lower than we'd like, it's actually a solid landing spot in a tight end room led by Evan Engram and featuring just one name younger than 27 years old (Caleb Lohner is 25, and he was a seventh-round pick last year). Joly hasn't turned 22 yet. Joly didn't test athletically, and he doesn't look like a game-changer on film due to pure speed and burst. Also, he wound up weighing 241 pounds at the NFL Combine despite being listed over 260 in his final year at North Carolina State. It just wasn't the best pre- draft process for Joly, and his stock dropped. However, if you want age-adjusted production, he has that. I mentioned the 88th-percentile Breakout Score already, and that pairs with 84th-percentile max yards per team attempt data. And the trajectory itself is intriguing. A high school wide receiver, Joly began his college career slowly with 18 catches as a freshman at Connecticut. As a sophomore, he led the team with 56 receptions for 578 yards and 2 touchdowns. He then transferred to North Carolina State, continuing his rise with a team-high 661 yards and 4 touchdowns on 43 catches while playing alongside KC Concepcion, a first-round pick in 2026, during the 2024 season. As a senior, Joly led the Wolfpack in receptions (49) and touchdown catches (7). His NFL career path just might come down to the draft capital. In the tight end ZAP Model, around 10% of prospects went on to log a best season (within their first three NFL years) of at least 10.0 PPR points per game. For players drafted between 100 and 200, that drops to around 6.4%. Of the ones who actually did put up good NFL seasons, they share stellar marks in max yards per team attempt. Excluding Jordan Cameron and Julius Thomas (who were basketball players in college), the hits from this draft tier averaged an 85th-percentile max yards per team attempt. Joly (84th) has that in his favor. The age-adjusted production and the landing spot in particular keep me reasonably optimistic about Joly's outlook in Denver. Dae'Quan Wright.lateround_profile: MISSISSIPPI DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Dae'Quan Wright began his college career at Virginia Tech, making a bit of an impact (19 catches for 208 yards) as a true freshman in 2022. He then saw his role expand as a Hokie in 2023 but wound up transferring to Ole Miss and finding paydirt nine times in 28 games while being more involved as a receiver. Overall, Wright's max yards per team attempt is a slight tick above the sample average, and his Breakout Score is about average. That's not bad, especially if he's as fast as his game tape makes him look. While he didn't run the 40 at the NFL Combine, his yards-after-catch ability and downfield attention are what separate his profile from many in this year's class. Wright easily leads this year's tight end class in career yards after the catch per reception, per PFF, and he's 11th (out of 27 combine-invited tight ends) in average depth of target - fractions of a yard away from a top-eight number. This all makes more sense when you learn that he had actually made some starts for Ole Miss as a wide receiver. That's a very important wrinkle to his profile, as we're obviously seeking receiving production at the NFL level. Given the time at wide receiver and enough positives in his profile, Wright has the potential to become a relevant tight end in the NFL for fantasy purposes, but it's hard to make him a true priority in rookie drafts. His blocking seems like enough of a concern that he could find it hard to earn reliable snaps, and it seems to be reducing his draft capital. From a fantasy football standpoint, he could be one of the bigger overperformers among this year's tight end class in the right situation. That situation being that his draft capital stays low (Round 6), but a team uses him as a big-bodied receiver and doesn't care that his blocking is below average. There's legitimate fantasy football potential here for Wright, but it's a profile that requires an NFL team buying into what he does best. Omar Cooper.lateround_profile: The best tackle-breaker in this wide receiver class is probably Omar Cooper. He avoided an absurd 0.31 tackles per reception across his career, a 97th percentile mark among ZAP Model wideouts since 2020. His efficiency profile is solid, too. Cooper posted a 79th percentile career yards per route run and an 82nd percentile first downs per route run. He split time between the slot and perimeter - a 48% career slot rate, per PFF - but was similarly effective in both alignments. The analytical concern comes from his trajectory. Cooper is a four-year player who didn't produce early, finishing with a 41.7 Breakout Score (55th percentile). That's not bad, but for a first-rounder, it's concerning. Of the 14 first-round wide receivers in the ZAP database with a Breakout Score below 50, just one has posted a 14-plus PPR points-per-game season within his first three years. And it was Kelvin Benjamin. To be fair, with 30th overall draft capital, Cooper is just as similar to earlier second-round picks as he is some first-round ones. And once you move into that range, the Breakout Score hit rate improves. Rashee Rice is a strong comp, and Deebo Samuel is another - Cooper is smaller, but several of his production metrics line up favorably with Samuel's collegiate profile. There's some minor versatility here, too. Cooper logged 97 rushing yards and a pair scores over his final two seasons and even returned a few kicks as a freshman at Indiana. He's unlikely to have that role in the NFL, but it's at least a signal of his athleticism. The bigger issue is landing spot. Cooper goes to the Jets - and, well, it's the Jets. Garrett Wilson is already a target hog there, and the team also spent a first-round pick on tight end Kenyon Sadiq. Geno Smith is projected to start in 2026, and while he should improve behind a better offensive line compared to last year, this still isn't an offense you're banking on for volume or efficiency. Cooper's profile looks more like a second-rounder than a true first, but it's workable. The concern is the short-term outlook - both in terms of earning targets and the quality of those targets. He just doesn't have the same kind of allure as the top-four wide receivers in this class. Analyticaly, at least. Chase Roberts.lateround_profile: BRIGHAM YOUNG DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk The last time a BYU wide receiver was drafted was 2023. You might've heard of him - Puka Nacua. Those are pretty large shoes to fill for Chase Roberts. Let's be clear, though: Roberts is almost certainly not the next Nacua. For starters, he'll be 25 at draft time. In ZAP Model history, only four wide receivers have entered the league at 25 or older. The most notable recent example is Devaughn Vele - and even that's more "solid depth piece" than fantasy cornerstone. Like Vele, Roberts is older largely because of a mission trip out of high school, which delayed his collegiate timeline. That context is important to know, but age still feeds directly into the model. Because of it, Roberts didn't register a Breakout Score. If we pretend he entered college immediately and was 23 instead of 25, his Breakout Score would've been 30.9, which isn't amazing anyway. At BYU, Roberts improved each season, peaking at a 2.39 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark in 2025. He was a reliable presence in the offense, finishing with the sixth-highest first downs per route run rate in this class. Roberts has solid size and played both inside and outside in college. Most impressively, his perimeter yards per route run was 2.61 - fifth-best in the class. That's not nothing. The age is what drags this profile down more than anything else. Without it, Roberts would still be more "interesting Day 3 flier" than priority target, but there'd be less friction.

Makai Lemon.lateround_profile: The Amon-Ra St. Brown comp for Makai Lemon may be a little played out, but it's still a quick way to frame his ceiling in fantasy. Both are technicians with elite short- area quickness and a relentless edge. Like St. Brown, Lemon has that dog in him. And like St. Brown, Lemon lived in the slot in college. His 75.8% career slot rate ranks in the 88th percentile among ZAP Model wideouts since 2020. That's rare territory for a first-rounder, too - only Jaylen Waddle, Kadarius Toney, Treylon Burks, Jaxon Smith- Njigba, and Emeka Egbuka have cleared a 70% slot rate since 2018. Fortunately, Lemon's profile separates from someone like Toney's pretty quickly. His career yards per route run sits at 3.02 (96th percentile), his first downs per route run is also in the 96th percentile, and he actually posted the best perimeter yards per route run in this year's class. On paper, that suggests he can win outside. The caveat: we've technically seen this archetype bust before. Since 2018, nine wideouts in the ZAP Model database picked in the top-100 have paired a 65% or better career slot rate with a perimeter yards per route run of at least 3.0. Lemon clears both with room to spare. Jaxon Smith-Njigba and Drake London are in that group - great. But so are Treylon Burks, Tutu Atwell, Elijah Moore, Mecole Hardman, Malachi Corley, Rondale Moore, and Jalin Hyatt. That's a mixed bag at best. The good news is that among first-rounders, the real comps are Burks, Smith-Njigba, and London. Two of those three have become elite NFL players. Lemon's peripherals also look stronger than Burks' did coming out. The bigger question is fit in Philadelphia. Jalen Hurts hasn't historically targeted the middle of the field at a high rate, which is where Lemon would ideally thrive. And since Hurts became the starter, the Eagles haven't posted a positive pass rate over expected in any season - volume isn't guaranteed. Philadelphia also drafted pass-catching tight end Eli Stowers in Round 2, and he'll likely occupy a similar area of the field as Lemon, limiting target upside. This pick does strongly signal that AJ Brown is on the way out, though. Lemon could step in as a top-two target alongside DeVonta Smith immediately. The pairing makes sense on paper, since Smith is a great field-stretcher and Lemon can win underneath. We just need the offense to evolve a bit for Lemon to truly hit, and that's no guarantee - it'd be nice to have a cleaner landing spot for such a talent. Eric McAlister.lateround_profile: TEXAS CHRISTIAN WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk Texas Christian wide receivers haven't exactly inspired confidence lately. Since 2020, we've seen Jalen Reagor, Derius Davis, Quentin Johnston, Savion Williams, and Jack Bech enter the league from there. There's a very real chance none of them ultimately outperform their draft-capital-based B2S expectation. Eric McAlister could be next up in late April's draft. Interestingly, though, he owns the second-best Breakout Score of that group, trailing only Quentin Johnston. And just barely - Johnston posted a 76.6, McAlister sits at 75.0. McAlister actually has the second-best Breakout Score in this entire class. That's either exciting...or mildly terrifying. Breakout Score is predictive, especially in the earlier portions of the draft. But once you move into the Day 3 range, chaos tends to take over. Draft capital itself barely matters after Pick 170 or 180. McAlister's Breakout Score is impressive. It would just be more impressive if he were projected inside the top 100 picks instead of Round 5 or 6. How did McAlister get there? Well, he spent two seasons at Boise State before transferring to TCU for his final two years. As a sophomore at Boise, he caught 47 passes for 873 yards and 5 touchdowns in just nine games. That prorated to a massive 3.56 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt (3.65 raw), which supercharged his Breakout Score. McAlister never quite reached that level again, but his final-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt of 2.78 was still strong - it would rank 10th in this class. Historically, Day 3 receivers with strong Breakout Scores have at least offered mild optimism. Among 230-plus wideouts drafted after Pick 100 from 2011 through 2023 (that gives them three-year NFL samples), only 25 posted a Breakout Score above 60. That group outperformed its draft-capital-based B2S expectation by 1.8 PPR points per game. The rest, predictably, underperformed. The odds of McAlister becoming a long-term fantasy staple are slim. Day 3 receivers always face steep odds. But if you're taking swings late in rookie drafts, betting on strong underlying production is rarely the wrong way to do it.

Antonio Williams.lateround_profile: In an underwhelming rookie class, Antonio Williams is at least somewhat interesting. Williams spent his entire collegiate career at Clemson and produced right away. As a freshman, he caught 56 passes for 604 yards and 4 touchdowns, leading the team in receiving. Clemson was fairly pass-heavy that season, though, so his 1.26 receiving yards per team pass attempt wasn't especially notable. Injuries limited him to five games as a sophomore. Then, over his final two seasons, Williams never exceeded 1.70 receiving yards per team pass attempt. Even when adjusted, his peak was just 1.82. Put differently, he was consistently productive, but never dominant. You'd like to see at least one standout season. His 38.4 Breakout Score reflects that - it's basically average. Historically, that kind of production hasn't translated well. Among Day 2 wide receivers since 2011, just 15 entered the league with a best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt below 2.0. Only Terry McLaurin and Ladd McConkey have become hits from that group. And McConkey - notably Williams' top comp - was incredibly efficient on a per- route basis. Williams doesn't have that going for him, which makes the profile harder to buy. Williams ran 78% of his routes from the slot, but Clemson did use him on the ground, which gives him a small bump in the ZAP Model. Top-100 picks with that kind of versatility tend to outperform expectation at a higher rate. The landing spot helps, especially in a class like this one where there aren't obvious directions to go in rookie drafts. Washington has very little established at wide receiver outside of Terry McLaurin, who turns 31 in September. Williams has a real chance to step in as the primary slot option right away and become an easy outlet for Jayden Daniels. And maybe there's a path to more - someone like McConkey has shown that a player with a similar frame can play inside and out at the next level. I just wouldn't bet on it. Donaven McCulley.lateround_profile: MICHIGAN DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Donaven McCulley's path to wide receiver is one of the more unique ones in this class. McCulley arrived at Indiana in 2021 as a quarterback. That experiment didn't last long - he completed just 35 of 82 pass attempts as a freshman before making the switch to wide receiver. Per a USA Today piece, McCulley noted that he'd grown taller and always had good hands, so he made the switch. The athleticism was obvious. The position just needed to change. Year 2, his first as a receiver, was understandably quiet. But in Year 3, we saw real progress: 48 receptions for 644 yards and a 1.91 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. For a converted quarterback, that's respectable. After appearing in just four games for Indiana in 2024, McCulley transferred to Michigan to close out his career. He slightly edged his previous efficiency mark, improving his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt by 0.02. According to Rich Hribar of Sharp Football Analysis, no wide receiver in this draft class had a higher uncatchable target rate than McCulley. That likely suppressed his counting stats and may have capped what could've been a slightly better Breakout Score (he finished at 34.8). At 6'5" with legitimate athletic traits, there's clearly clay to mold here. The issue is that the underlying efficiency never truly popped. His career yards per route run sits at 1.56. And that wasn't meaningfully better on the perimeter, where he's almost certainly destined to line up in the NFL. It's a fun story and an intriguing athletic bet. A team could absolutely take a late-round swing on the size and developmental upside. But from a fantasy perspective, this is still a long-range projection rather than a profile built on proven production.

Le'Veon Moss.lateround_profile: TEXAS A&M DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk The issue with Le'Veon Moss isn't the name - it absolutely sounds like an NFL running back's name. It's the production. Moss spent four years at Texas A&M and never once handled more than 25% of his team's rush attempts in a season. His best-season attempt share was only higher than Eli Heidenreich's in this class, and Heidenreich is basically a slot receiver playing running back. The receiving profile doesn't bail Moss out. He posted just a 6.7% best-season reception share, the third-lowest mark among Combine invite running backs. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt came in at 0.63, well below the class average. So we've got a limited rushing share and limited receiving involvement. At the Combine, Moss weighed 203 pounds. That's not tiny, but it's also not the kind of frame that screams "early-down hammer" - especially when the production doesn't support that label. Historically, that combination is rough. In the ZAP Model database, only four running backs have reached 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons with both a sub-8% best-season reception share and a sub-30% best-season attempt share: Chris Carson, Dameon Pierce, Damien Harris, and CJ Anderson. Pierce and Harris had decent draft capital. All four of them were over 215 pounds entering the league. Moss doesn't check either of those boxes. Which brings us to the key question: where does Moss fit? He doesn't project as a clear passing-down option. He doesn't profile as a high-volume early-down grinder. He didn't dominate touches in college. And as a likely Day 3 pick, he won't have investment to force opportunity. That's an uncomfortable place to live for fantasy purposes. Without a standout trait or a defined role to lean on, Moss feels more like depth than a back you'll be excited to roster.

Emmanuel Henderson.lateround_profile: KANSAS WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk Among the 46 wide receivers invited to this year's NFL Combine, Emmanuel Henderson ranked second with a 40.5% career deep-ball target rate. He was also fourth in collegiate average depth of target. What's interesting is that Henderson wasn't strictly a boundary-only player. He ran about 36% of his routes from the slot, which is a fairly healthy rate for someone with that kind of downfield profile. Henderson's college path took him to Alabama for three seasons before transferring to Kansas for his final year. At Alabama, there was essentially no meaningful receiving production - he caught just five passes across three seasons. His final year there resulted in an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate of 0.28. Kansas is where things finally clicked. In 2025, he caught 45 passes for 766 yards and 5 touchdowns, posting a 2.39 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt. That was enough to get him on the board with a Breakout Score, but it was just 21.1. So we've got one productive season, and it came late. Henderson also contributed in the return game, bringing back 18 kicks for 455 yards and a touchdown in his final season. That special teams juice could play a role in NFL outlook. But from a fantasy perspective, this is a historically fragile archetype. Since 2020, 17 wide receivers have entered the league with a deep-ball target rate of 35% or higher. Henderson was north of 40%. None of those 17 averaged 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. When a player's offensive profile is heavily tilted toward vertical shots, it usually signals a limited route tree and volatile usage. Henderson could carve out a role as a speed-based rotational receiver and special teams contributor. That can absolutely help an NFL team. Fantasy managers, though, shouldn't expect much beyond the occasional splash play.

DJ Rogers.lateround_profile: TEXAS CHRISTIAN DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk DJ Rogers isn't really registering on the tight end model much at all. He played five years at TCU but didn't catch more than 12 passes until his fifth and final year, when he caught 34 balls for 319 yards (9.4 yards per reception) and 2 touchdowns. In total, Rogers averaged under 1.4 yards per route run over his final three seasons, per PFF, and he was an underneath receiver by average depth of target, even after adjusting for team context. Despite that profile - which includes some size and athleticism - film and scouting grades aren't in agreement with his baseline blocking ability, generally skewing toward the pessimistic end of the spectrum. His lack of a good Breakout Score or peak receiving season hurts his potential for future fantasy football success. Profiles similar to Rogers' occasionally hit with some tangential fantasy relevance but rarely are tied to consistent fantasy production. Rogers weighed in at 258 pounds at the Combine, third-heaviest in the class (out of 27 Combine-invited tight ends), and a long arm measurement could get a team interested solely based on his size. Unfortunately, he didn't run the 40 at the Combine, so maybe a great pro day 40 vaults him up the draft board a bit, but as it is, Rogers is likely a fantasy afterthought who would spend more time blocking than running routes for an NFL team. Tanner Koziol.lateround_profile: Tanner Koziol was the 12th tight end off the board in the 2026 NFL Draft and actually the second for the Jaguars, who selected blocking specialist Nate Boerkircher 56th overall. Koziol projects to be much more of a pass-catcher between the two, which is why the fantasy-focused ZAP Model likes him more than Boerkircher despite a massive difference in their draft capital. Koziol actually led two different offenses in receptions in his final two years of college, Ball State and Houston, respectively. He also pops in terms of an interesting stat: first downs per route run. Koziol might be a ball player. In total, he owns a Breakout Score and a max yards per team attempt mark at or above the 85th percentile in the model. That's good stuff. It's not just production, either. He crosses the threshold for viable athleticism. Koziol clocked a 4.70-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine at 6'7" and 247 pounds, good for a 105 height-adjusted Speed Score. He also graded out with a really good weight-adjusted burst score (vertical and broad jump). Although those aren't direct inputs in the model, it's nice to see there's more than just straight-line speed, especially given the lighter frame. It's also looking like there just isn't much competition for Koziol after this season in Jacksonville, either. Brenton Strange, Quintin Morris, and Hunter Long have contracts that run out after 2026. It's still late draft capital and not a ton of investment in Koziol. With that said, the names that do hit when drafted this late have possessed elite results in max yards per team attempt, and Koziol checks that box. Brenen Thompson.lateround_profile: The ZAP Model doesn't love Brenen Thompson because he's 164 pounds and had a Breakout Score of 35.4, a 41st percentile tally. In the ZAP Model database, the only sub- 180-pound wide receivers to post a 15-plus PPR points-per-game season within their first three years are first-rounders DeVonta Smith and Jordan Addison. Smaller receivers can still offer usable production - think Josh Downs or Darnell Mooney - but many of those mini-hits came with significantly stronger Breakout Scores than Thompson's. But screw it. This is a fun player. Thompson spent one year at Texas, two at Oklahoma, and one at Mississippi State, where he finished his college career. He didn't eclipse 250 receiving yards in a season until that final campaign, when he broke out for 57 receptions, 1,054 yards, and 6 touchdowns. His size also shouldn't automatically remove him from consideration. Interestingly, he barely played in the slot in college, running just 12.2% of his routes there, per PFF. On only 86 slot routes, though, he posted a ridiculous 4.15 yards per route run, the highest mark in the class. It's a small sample, but it hints at some untapped deployment flexibility. And then there's the 4.26 speed. Thompson's ability to stress defenses vertically - he posted the fifth-highest deep-ball target rate in the class - is something the Chargers will try to utilize. New offensive coordinator Mike McDaniel loves speed, and there's no doubt in my mind that he played a role in selecting Thompson. The Chargers aren't exactly loaded at wide receiver, either. Ladd McConkey is a beast, but Quentin Johnston still has plenty of question marks attached to him, and Tre Harris didn't get a lot done as a rookie. Thompson, at least, can play the role of burner in that offense, likely in heavier wide receiver packages as a rookie. The odds of Thompson becoming a high-volume receiver are small - just being honest. We don't see this kind of profile hit very often. But the pairing with McDaniel makes him more intriguing than he would've been elsewhere. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 56

Jalen Walthall.lateround_profile: INCARNATE WORD DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk No player in the ZAP Model database had come from Incarnate Word. Until now with Jalen Walthall. That's where he spent two of his four collegiate seasons. The other two? Hawaii. What a wild, wild collegiate journey for a player looking to be drafted to the NFL. At Hawaii, Walthall's production never got off the ground. As a freshman and sophomore, he posted sub-1.0 prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt rates. He appeared in just five games during his second year, but even on a full-season pace, the numbers weren't trending toward anything meaningful. Walthall then transferred to Incarnate Word, where the level of competition dipped. Even there, though, his best receiving yards per team pass attempt rate topped out at 2.39. And once that was adjusted for strength of opponent, it wasn't enough to earn a Breakout Score. That's a major hurdle. In the ZAP Model database, 61 wide receivers have posted a sub-1.5 best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate. Walthall falls into that group. Of those 61, only Tyreek Hill and Hunter Renfrow managed to score more than 12 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. Hill, as you've read in other blurbs, had elite speed and rushing production to buoy his profile. Renfrow's peak was modest and short-lived. The odds are overwhelmingly against this turning into something meaningful for fantasy. Walthall's path has been unique, but analytically, it's a long shot bet.

Noah Whittington.lateround_profile: OREGON DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Noah Whittington played six seasons of college football and never reached 1,000 total yards in a single year. Whittington's most encouraging stretch likely came in Year 3, his first season at Oregon after two years at Western Kentucky. That year, he posted his best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate and his second-best adjusted total yards per team play mark. He also tied his career-high reception share at 7.1%. And he did that in the same offense as Bucky Irving. You'd hope the arrow would continue pointing up from there, but it didn't. Whittington tore his ACL in 2023, the following season, and the profile never really recovered. His best-season reception share ranked bottom-five in the class. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was also bottom-five. His top adjusted total yards per team play mark - 1.20 - ranked third-worst among Combine-invited running backs. Even if we soften the thresholds by 20% - pushing them to roughly an 8.5% reception share, 1.45 total yards per team play, and 0.50 receiving yards per team pass attempt - Whittington still falls well short. In the ZAP Model database, 66 running backs have failed to meet those boosted baselines. Just two - Andre Ellington and Chris Carson - produced even one fantasy-relevant season in their first three years. That's not encouraging. The profile is iffy, and the lack of any true ceiling season makes it difficult to sell Whittington as anything more than a depth guy. As always with running backs, landing spot can manufacture short-term opportunity. But analytically, this is not a profile you'd want to aggressively bet on.

Aaron Anderson.lateround_profile: LOUISIANA STATE DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk There are four LSU wide receivers at the NFL Combine this year, but all of them are projected to go on Day 3 - including Aaron Anderson. Anderson began his career at Alabama, redshirting as a freshman before transferring to LSU. He then spent three seasons in Baton Rouge, where his production profile settled in as below average. His best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was 1.88, which translated to a 25.5 Breakout Score. That's not moving the needle much analytically. To his credit, Anderson posted the best yards per route run figures among the LSU wideouts in this class. His 2.07 career yards per route run is slightly above the class average among Combine invites. But that efficiency came almost entirely from the slot - he ran over 88% of his routes from there, the second-highest slot rate in the class. That archetype hasn't treated us well recently. Since 2020, there have been 12 wide receivers in the ZAP Model database with a Breakout Score below 40 and a career slot rate above 80%. None of them have reached 10 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. And two of those players were top-100 picks. Anderson likely won't get that kind of draft capital boost. It's hard to see a clear path to fantasy relevance here. Anderson could carve out a depth role as a slot specialist, but from a rookie draft perspective, this isn't a profile you need to prioritize.

Jeff Caldwell.lateround_profile: CINCINNATI DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Jeff Caldwell might be the biggest size-speed outlier in this entire class. He's 6'5", 216 pounds, and ran a 4.31 at the Combine. On top of that, he posted the second-best broad jump and vertical among wide receiver invitees. That's absurd movement at that frame. Those testing numbers probably shifted him from fringe-undrafted territory into "someone will take a shot" range. But Caldwell's outlook is almost entirely traits-based. Caldwell began his career at Lindenwood in 2022, and he stayed there for three seasons. On the surface, a 3.24 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark in 2024 looks impressive. Once adjusted for strength of competition, though, it falls below 2.0. We have to always remember that raw dominance at a lower level doesn't carry the same signal. Caldwell transferred to Cincinnati for his final season and finished with 32 catches for 478 yards and 6 touchdowns. His 1.31 receiving yards per team pass attempt there didn't move the needle analytically. The result is a 29th percentile Breakout Score and a best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt of 1.94. That's not the kind of production profile that typically overcomes mid-to-late-round draft capital. However, there's no denying the athletic profile. Caldwell's one of the best athletes we've seen come out of the Combine. It's rare. But historically, betting on testing without meaningful, translatable production is tough. Unless draft capital comes in far stronger than expected, Caldwell is more of a last-round rookie draft dart throw than someone you need to prioritize.

Ja'Kobi Lane.lateround_profile: Ja'Kobi Lane is one of just five early-declare wide receivers in this year's class, spending all three collegiate seasons at USC. After catching just seven passes in six games as a freshman, he jumped to 43 and then 49 receptions over the next two years. The production profile, though, is underwhelming. His 2024 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 1.14, rising to 2.35 as a junior. Even that peak ranked outside the top 20 in the class. The result is a 35.9 Breakout Score - a below-average mark in the ZAP Model. To be fair, Lane shared the field with likely first-rounder Makai Lemon, so Teammate Score gives him a modest boost. But even with that context, the résumé looks pretty ordinary. His career yards per route run, per PFF, ranks 26th in the class. He was fine at generating first downs, but nothing here signals a difference-maker. While he has length, Lane's frame is leaner than you'd expect for a true contested-catch specialist - and that was a big part of his profile. Among this year's Combine invitees, he ranked first with a near 33% contested target rate. With top-100 draft capital, he's just the fourth wide receiver since 2020 to enter the league with a contested target rate above 30%. The other three are Josh Palmer, Nico Collins, and Alec Pierce - not bad company. But each of those players had stronger Breakout Scores and more size to support that play style. Lane's contested usage seems to stem more from an inability to separate. In Baltimore, Lane is likely buried early. Zay Flowers and Rashod Bateman are ahead of him, with Bateman under contract through 2027 and Flowers potentially there long-term as well. The Ravens also drafted Elijah Sarratt, who may just be the better player. And even with a new coordinator, this is still an offense that hasn't posted a positive pass rate over expected since Lamar Jackson became the starter in 2019. Volume is unlikely to be a strength. There are a couple of things to like - mainly his age and early declare status - but this isn't a profile that demands attention in rookie drafts. Chris Hilton.lateround_profile: LOUISIANA STATE DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk All four LSU wide receivers invited to this year's Combine checked in under 200 pounds - Aaron Anderson was the heaviest at just 191. Chris Hilton fits the mold, and with that lighter frame came good speed. He ran a 4.41 40-yard dash, which is a strong time. That's about where the positives taper off. Hilton spent five seasons at LSU - credit to him for staying put in the portal era - but he never topped 250 receiving yards in a single year. As you'd expect, that means he failed to register a Breakout Score. Hilton's role was extremely narrow. Per PFF, over 42% of his targets came on deep-ball throws, the highest rate in this class. He was essentially a lid-lifter. The problem is that he was rarely a focal point. He never reached a double-digit prorated target share in any season, giving him the lowest best-season target share among Combine invite wide receivers. When you combine the lack of volume, the one-dimensional usage, and the absence of any meaningful production, you're left with a very rough fantasy projection. LSU's receiver room has been great in recent years, so that context is important to know. But projecting that profile forward - into a league where the competition only gets tougher - makes it difficult to see a path to consistent fantasy relevance.

Denzel Boston.lateround_profile: The expectation was that Denzel Boston would be a first-round pick. He didn't get that top-32 capital, but he still landed early in Round 2 with Cleveland. Boston spent all four years at Washington, sharing the field with Rome Odunze, Jalen McMillan, and Ja'Lynn Polk during his first two seasons. Naturally, his production was limited during that stretch. Even accounting for that, his eventual breakout was a bit underwhelming. In 2024, Boston posted a 2.00 receiving yards per team pass attempt rate. That climbed to 2.58 the following season - 2.75 when adjusted for strength of schedule. The result was a 43.8 Breakout Score, which sits around the 60th percentile. That's more acceptable for a second-rounder than a mid-first-round prospect, where some projected him back in March. But you'd still prefer to see a stronger mark. Beyond Breakout Score, the analytical profile doesn't offer much help. Boston's career yards per route run is just 2.02, a 48th percentile figure. Since 2020, 29 Day 2 wide receivers have posted a career mark below 2.20. Only Nico Collins and Jayden Reed from that group managed to score more than 13 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. The landing spot doesn't do him many favors, either. Cleveland used a first-round pick on the more well-rounded KC Concepcion, and Boston will have to compete with him throughout his rookie contract. It's also an offense with a featured tight end and, most likely, an unsettled quarterback situation. Boston would've been an easy fade had he gone in Round 1, given where his rookie draft cost would've landed. Now, the market will probably be less aggressive. Just understand that this is a highly volatile profile. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 42

Barion Brown.lateround_profile: The Saints kept adding weapons in this draft, selecting Barion Brown in Round 6. He's getting a profile update because the ZAP Model...oddly doesn't mind him. Brown is fast - legitimately fast. A former track athlete, he was heavily involved as a returner across four seasons - three at Kentucky and one at LSU - and he backed up the speed with a 4.40 forty at the Combine. All things considered, the production profile isn't terrible. At his size and with that speed, Brown likely projects as a gadget or space-oriented receiver at the next level. Historically, those players haven't been consistent fantasy contributors. But there's at least something here. Brown posted a 1.97 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark as a freshman - the best of his career - which helped generate a 47.6 Breakout Score (66th percentile). That's a solid number. His collegiate usage matches the archetype. Brown ranked top-10 among Combine wide receivers in both behind-the-line and deep target rates, and about 18% of his receiving yards came on screens - also a top-10 figure in the class. In other words, most of his touches were manufactured or came on vertical shots. That doesn't point to a refined route runner - it suggests a player offenses tried to scheme into space or send deep to create big plays. Again...speed. There's intrigue because that kind of athleticism is rare. But this type of wide receiver usually needs overwhelming traits to truly hit in fantasy. New Orleans clearly leaned into that idea with their Day 3 receivers, adding both Bryce Lance and Brown as potential big- play specialists. Brown is the type of player you can take at the end of rookie drafts and stash on a taxi squad. With the ZAP Model more optimistic than his draft slot suggests, there's at least a non-zero chance he does something at the next level. Adam Randall.lateround_profile: We seem to get a wide receiver-to-running back convert in every draft class. This year, it's Adam Randall. The key difference between Randall and recent converts like Brashard Smith and Tyrone Tracy is size. Randall checks in north of 230 pounds. In ZAP Model history, he's just one of 11 backs at that weight to post a best-season reception share above 10%. Most of the players in that bucket - Saquon Barkley, Leonard Fournette, Eddie Lacy, Jeremy Hill, James Conner, Najee Harris - came with strong draft capital. The ones who didn't haven't given us much at the NFL level. That's where things get tricky. Randall's profile is easy to like on the surface: big-bodied former receiver, positional versatility, untapped upside. But the underlying data is underwhelming. His reception share clears 10%, but his best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt is just 0.59 - seventh-worst in the class. His adjusted total yards per team play ranks fifth-worst. He only spent one of his four collegiate seasons at Clemson playing running back, logging 168 carries in 2025. With that kind of limited experience, you wouldn't expect a polished profile. And you don't get one. Despite the size, Randall ranked below average in both missed tackles forced per attempt and yards after contact per attempt. For a player built like a power back, the power metrics don't stand out. Interestingly, the landing spot might actually help, even if it doesn't look that way at face value. Most wouldn't be excited about a back sitting behind Derrick Henry, but for Randall, it's probably ideal. He needs time to learn the position, and there's no better way to do so than behind a future Hall of Famer. Randall's still under 22 and very raw as a running back. In a class lacking obvious upside swings, Randall is at least worth keeping on your radar in the later rounds of rookie drafts. Josh Cameron.lateround_profile: BAYLOR WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk Josh Cameron often looks like a running back playing wide receiver. He's got a thick, sturdy build, and Baylor leaned into that across his five seasons there. He wasn't used out of the backfield much, but he also wasn't asked to win deep - his career deep-ball target rate was the second-lowest in this class. His average depth of target sat a bit higher because he lived in that intermediate range. Cameron is more of a traits bet than a production one. RJ Maryland.lateround_profile: SOUTHERN METHODIST DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Based on age- and schedule-adjusted data, RJ Maryland is one of the better receiving tight ends in this year's class - and, frankly, in the entire tight end database. But you wouldn't know it based on the pre-draft hype, which is why the ZAP Score and the comps are uninspiring. Maryland, the son of Russell Maryland (the first overall pick in the 1991 NFL Draft), is projected to go undrafted most places you look. Maryland tore his ACL during the 2024 season, ending with just seven games played. But he bounced back with a solid senior year and ran a 4.51-second 40-yard dash, tied for second- fastest among runners in this year's class. We have to point out that Maryland weighed in as the second-lightest tight end at the Combine at just 236 pounds. His weight-adjusted vertical was also poor, yet we've seen plenty of other pass-catching tight ends play well despite that being the case (Zach Ertz, OJ Howard, Dalton Schultz types) though those players were all bigger and heavier. Let's look at some of that production I've mentioned. Maryland spent all four college seasons at Southern Methodist, churning out 113 catches, 1,495 yards, and 19 touchdowns in 46 games, finishing with a career 13.2 yards per catch rate. He also maintained at least 1.60 yards per route run, per PFF, for each of the last three seasons: really solid for a tight end. And for additional context here, Maryland put up these good numbers sharing time with Jordan Hudson (a Combine-invited receiver), Romello Brinson (a draft-eligible receiver), and Matthew Hibner (a Combine-invited tight end). Maryland was still pretty productive as a freshman with Rashee Rice logging 96 catches for 1,355 yards in 2022. But if none of this age-adjusted data and a still-young draft age (he won't turn 22 until July) is getting NFL teams excited, then there's not much we can do. Maryland is small, even compared to a light tight end class. He may not transition to a plus blocker in the NFL, which he wasn't able to do in college. That might keep NFL teams from investing in his skillset. Jam Miller.lateround_profile: ALABAMA DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Jam Miller flashed some juice at the NFL Combine. At 209 pounds, he ran a 4.42 40, giving him a Speed Score just shy of 110. That might be the best part of the profile, if we're being honest. Miller spent four seasons at Alabama and never reached 150 carries in a year. His best rush attempt share season topped out at 29.7%. Jordyn Tyson.lateround_profile: There's a real chance the ZAP Model is too bullish on Jordyn Tyson - but within the predictive analytics we care about, he looks the part. Outside of draft capital, Breakout Score carries the most weight in the wide receiver ZAP Model by a wide margin. Tyson's mark checks in at 95.1, the best in this year's class and good for the 99th percentile. That's largely because he produced at a high level throughout his collegiate career. As a young freshman at Colorado, Tyson led the team in receiving in just nine games. A devastating knee injury cut that season short and wiped out his second year, but he bounced back in Year 3 with a prorated adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate of 3.58 - the best top-season mark in this class. This is a rare kind of Breakout Score. Only nine ZAP Model wide receivers drafted in the top 20 have posted a mark above 90: Mike Evans, Sammy Watkins, Amari Cooper, Corey Davis, CeeDee Lamb, Ja'Marr Chase, Chris Olave, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, and Malik Nabers. That's elite company - and Tyson got there as a four-year player, a distinction only Olave and Davis share. The elephant in the room is the injury history. There's the knee, a broken collarbone, and a hamstring issue. You can subjectively ding his ZAP score because of it, but he's already returned from the knee and collarbone injuries, and the hamstring appears to be behind him. Some of the peripherals aren't perfect, either. Tyson has a bit of a yards-after-the- catch problem, and per PFF, he ranks in just the 7th percentile in avoided tackles per reception. That's not disqualifying - players like Chris Olave and DeVonta Smith graded poorly there, too - but it's not a plus. The Saints selected Tyson eighth overall, and it's a solid landing spot. He can line up all over the formation - he posted a 31% career slot rate in college - and, as Matt Harmon has shown with Reception Perception, Tyson thrives against zone coverage. Olave wins against man. That combination could stress defenses, and with little proven depth behind them, both receivers have a path to meaningful volume. Kellen Moore's offenses have also tended to play fast, which helps on the volume front. As an Olave truther, it's wild to say this, but Tyson has a path to eventually being the more impactful fantasy player. It probably won't happen right away, but the ceiling is there. Zachariah Branch.lateround_profile: Back in March, I thought Zachariah Branch might be the player I'd fade most in rookie drafts this year. But given how this class shook out, he's almost...interesting. There are some positives. Branch is an early declare, and the model gives him credit for his versatility and unique usage. He's explosive, shifty, and can operate in short areas. The negatives? There are plenty. Branch never truly produced at a high level. His raw receiving yards per team pass attempt failed to reach 2.0 in a season, and even when adjusted for strength of schedule, his peak was just 2.11. That led to a below-average 31.2 Breakout Score. The usage profile is even more concerning. A massive 45.5% of his career targets came behind the line of scrimmage. That doesn't mean he can't develop into something more, but it does mean you're betting on a significant leap. Historically, that's been a dangerous bet. With top-100 draft capital, Branch becomes the 11th wide receiver since 2020 to enter the league with a career behind-the-line target rate above 30% - and the first to exceed 40%. Only two players in that group, Wan'Dale Robinson and Luther Burden, posted Breakout Scores above 75. The rest? Lynn Bowden, Amari Rodgers, Anthony Schwartz, Kadarius Toney, Rondale Moore, Velus Jones, Tre Tucker, and Malachi Corley. A big reason for that is screen-heavy usage. Over 35% of Branch's career yards came on screens. Among top-100 picks since 2020, only two receivers have cleared 30% of their career yardage from screens: Malachi Corley and Anthony Schwartz. This is an archetype that's burned teams before. To be fair, though, the athleticism is intriguing, and the landing spot could've been worse. Atlanta's depth chart entering the draft featured Drake London (a stud), Jahan Dotson (not a stud), and Olamide Zaccheaus (not a stud). Branch will likely start in a gadget role, but it wouldn't be surprising if he earns more meaningful reps by the end of the 2026 season than he would have on most teams. Still, this is a tough profile to buy into. In a stronger class, Branch would be closer to an avoid than a target. But in this one, where you're throwing darts earlier than usual, he's at least in the mix. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 46

Seth McGowan.lateround_profile: KENTUCKY WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk Prior to this draft class, only one ZAP Model running back - Martez Carter - entered the NFL at age 25 or older. This year, we have two. One of them is Seth McGowan. Does age matter? Yes. The model accounts for it, and historically, older prospects have produced weaker outcomes. But context is needed. College football has shifted dramatically in recent years. Transfer rules changed. The pandemic extended eligibility. NIL deals altered incentives. As a result, we're seeing older backs more frequently. Over the last two draft classes (excluding this one), 10 running backs either attended the NFL Combine or were drafted at age 24 or older. From 2011 through 2023, there were 11 total. Last year alone, RJ Harvey, Woody Marks, and Jacory Croskey-Merritt were all 24 or older at selection. Adjusted for draft capital, the hit rates for these older prospects haven't been disastrous of late. As long as the production profiles were strong. McGowan's profile isn't bad - it's just uneven. He began at Oklahoma in 2020 before being dismissed following legal issues, though the charges were ultimately dropped. After time away from the game, he resurfaced at Butler Community College in 2023, then transferred to New Mexico State in 2024. There, he logged 152 carries in a backfield that also featured 2026 draft hopeful Mike Washington. More importantly for his analytical outlook, McGowan posted a career-best 16.3% reception share that year. That season did a lot of the heavy lifting for his pass-catching profile, showing at least some ability to function on passing downs. The question is sustainability. When a receiving spike comes after multiple stops and years in the wilderness, it's fair to wonder how sticky it really is. In 2025, McGowan transferred again - this time to Kentucky. There, he carried the ball 165 times, recorded a 9.1% reception share, and posted a career-best 1.22 adjusted total yards per team play. McGowan is a big, physical back who plays through contact. Among this year's Combine invitees, he ranks top-five in career missed tackles forced per attempt, per PFF. His explosive run rate is average at best, but when you pair the tackle-breaking ability with an acceptable receiving profile, you can at least envision a path where he sticks as a quality backup with contingent upside.

His adjusted receiving yards per team play also ranked second. And his best-season adjusted total yards per team play - which includes rushing - came in at 2.10, a mark only two running backs in the class topped. From a pure production standpoint, Reid is near the top of this group. The problem is the body type. In the ZAP Model database, there are 23 running backs under 190 pounds. Only De'Von Achane - who added weight once he reached the NFL - and Tarik Cohen have delivered truly fantasy-relevant seasons. You could maybe toss Keaton Mitchell into the conversation, but that's not exactly a deep bench of hits. So the bet with Reid is pretty clear: you're hoping for a creative offensive coordinator who leans into his receiving ability and builds a role around him. In the right system, he could be a PPR weapon. The challenge is that the NFL has shifted. Running back target spikes aren't quite what they were in the Tarik Cohen era. It's harder now for a sub-180-pound back to live purely off receiving volume without a lot of rushing work. Pitt allegiance aside - that's my alma mater, for those of you who don't know - Reid is fun. He's quick, he's productive as a receiver, and he's electric in space. But fun doesn't automatically translate to fantasy difference-maker - especially at 174 pounds.

Malachi Fields.lateround_profile: Malachi Fields' production profile is lacking. He began his collegiate career at Virginia in 2021, catching just 11 passes as a true freshman before a foot injury limited him to one game as a sophomore. His junior season showed some promise - 58 receptions for 811 yards and 5 touchdowns - resulting in a 2.24 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. That ended up being his best year. Fields played two more seasons - one at Virginia and one at Notre Dame - but neither produced a stronger campaign. Now, a little behind-the-scenes note: in the pre-draft version of the guide, Fields had the wrong birthday in the ZAP Model, which lowered his Breakout Score by about 10 points. So, yes, he looks better now than he did then. He still doesn't look great. Fields' Breakout Score comes in at a very average 43.1, and the ZAP Model still tags him as a high-risk prospect. The advanced metrics back that up. His career yards per route run sits at 1.94 - a major concern. Since 2020, 18 Day 2 wide receivers have entered the league with a career mark below 2.0. None of them have produced more than 12.5 PPR points per game in any of their first three seasons. There are some positives. Fields has prototypical size, and there's opportunity in the Giants offense, where Malik Nabers is the only long-term fixture. If you really want to stretch for an outcome, you can squint and see something like Michael Pittman, one of his top-three comps. But that's closer to a ceiling than an expectation. Colbie Young.lateround_profile: Only six wide receivers in the ZAP Model database have averaged at least 14 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons after being drafted outside the top 100: Allen Hurns, Martavis Bryant, Tyreek Hill, Hunter Renfrow, Amon-Ra St. Brown, and Puka Nacua. That's rare air. And there's no consistent size archetype among them. So while Colbie Young checking in at 6'5", 218 pounds may have appealed to Cincinnati, it doesn't really move the needle for fantasy. Young ends up as a Day 3 pick out of Georgia, where he spent two seasons after stops at Miami and Lackawanna College. Across five years, the production never truly materialized. Even at Lackawanna, he posted just a 1.68 prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt mark as a freshman - a number that fades even further once adjusted for competition. There are a few intriguing traits. Young posted elevated deep-ball and behind-the-line target rates, suggesting teams were trying to manufacture touches. He also forced missed tackles at an above-average clip, hinting at some ability after the catch. But zooming out, this is a long-shot profile. Day 3 receivers without meaningful production or a Breakout Score rarely become fantasy difference-makers. Elijah Sarratt.lateround_profile: The Ravens took Ja'Kobi Lane on Day 2 and followed it up with Elijah Sarratt in Round 4. That's later than his projected Round 2 to Round 3 capital, so his statistical comps may look a bit more optimistic than they should. There's a real argument that Sarratt is the better prospect, though. After beginning his career at Saint Francis as a zero-star recruit, he finished with a 51.8 Breakout Score - a 71st percentile mark and one of the stronger figures in this class. That's largely due to his consistency. Across his three non-Saint Francis seasons - one at James Madison and two at Indiana - Sarratt posted an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt between 2.58 and 2.75 each year. He's one of just three receivers in this class with multiple seasons above 2.5 - and the only one to do it three times. The efficiency metrics back it up. Sarratt had a 2.56 career yards per route run, a top- six mark in the class, and generated a first down on 12.5% of his routes, trailing only Makai Lemon. There may be some separation concerns, though - similar to what you could argue with his new teammate, Ja'Kobi Lane. And that's highlighted in a high (95th percentile) rate of seeing contested targets. That likely explains some of the comps, like Michael Thomas and Pat Bryant - bigger receivers who have spent time operating from the slot in the NFL. Thomas obviously became much more than that, but it's fair to wonder if Sarratt settles into a big-slot type of role. Overall, in a class full of middling age-adjusted production profiles, Sarratt's stands out. The issue is the landing spot. Baltimore hasn't posted a positive pass rate over expected in any season since Lamar Jackson became the starter, and the target competition is just strong enough to limit Sarratt's path to volume. Just don't be surprised if Sarratt ends up being the best wide receiver from this 2026 class for the Ravens. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 53

Nick Singleton.lateround_profile: The Senior Bowl is invaluable for evaluating prospects up close. Unfortunately, injuries are part of the deal. That's what happened to Penn State's Nick Singleton, who broke his foot during practice in January. It may have contributed to his slide to Pick 165, where Tennessee scooped him up. According to the ZAP Model, that could be a value. Among Combine-invited running backs, Singleton ranks sixth in best-season prorated reception share, fifth in adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt, and sixth in adjusted total yards per team play - out of 20 players. That's even more impressive given that he shared a backfield with Kaytron Allen for all four seasons at Penn State. Singleton was clearly the better pass-catcher, highlighted by a 41-catch season in 2024 compared to Allen's 18. There are some concerns outside the model. Per PFF, Singleton forced missed tackles at a below-average rate, and despite strong athletic traits, his rate of runs gaining 10 or more yards was below average among Combine invitees. The tools are there - the consistency wasn't always. Still, this is a rare profile for a Day 3 pick. In ZAP Model history, just 13 running backs drafted after Pick 100 have entered the league with a prorated reception share above 12%, a best-season total yards per team play above 1.6, and an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt above 1.0. Singleton cleared all three with room to spare. Of those 13 backs, 9 exceeded their draft-capital-based expectation, and on average, the group outperformed expectation by 3.5 PPR points per game. When Day 3 running backs hit, it's often because they land in ambiguous backfields. You can see that reflected in seasonal average draft position - when a team's top running back isn't a high-investment player, it signals opportunity. That's exactly the situation in Tennessee. Tyjae Spears and Tony Pollard are both set to hit free agency next year, and there's no clear long-term answer in place. There may not even be a short-term one in place. Singleton is absolutely worth a look in rookie drafts. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 89

Eli Raridon.lateround_profile: Eli Raridon was the ninth tight end drafted within the first 95 picks of the 2026 NFL Draft, which really put things into context. Raridon played four seasons at Notre Dame, yet despite his size, he scored just 3 times on 48 catches over 40 games, and there are some blocking concerns. That didn't seem to affect his draft stock too much, though. The Patriots seem good with the upside. Raridon is 70th-percentile or better in max yards per team attempt and Breakout Score among the tight end database, and there's still a bit of that big-bodied, basketball- background archetype in Raridon's profile that we've seen lead to some really nice fantasy football production in the past. We shouldn't ignore him (the NFL clearly didn't), and there's a lot of room for him in New England's tight end room. Hunter Henry is 32 years old and has just one more year on his contract. Julian Hill was an undrafted free agent from the 2023 class. Other than that, Raridon should get chances. He's a good athlete (110 height-adjusted Speed Score), he's big, and he is going to be playing alongside an MVP-caliber quarterback in Drake Maye. It's a promising landing spot for a prospect with fantasy football potential in a class with some questionable team fits and some reaches on primary blocking tight ends. They bought into the offense with Jordyn Tyson in Round 1, and while Juwan Johnson and Noah Fant are each under contract for two more years, they're 30 and 29 already, respectively. Delp likely isn't projecting for a huge rookie season, but in a few years, he could be a cornerstone for the Saints' offense. Kendrick Law.lateround_profile: KENTUCKY WAIVER WIRE ADD Low Risk Neutral High Risk The ZAP Model isn't fond of Kendrick Law. He failed to register a Breakout Score, and he's not expected to go early in the draft. But his profile is undeniably unique. Law spent three seasons at Alabama doing very little as a receiver - just 33 total catches and no meaningful efficiency spike. He transferred to Kentucky in 2025 and had his most productive year, catching 53 passes for 540 yards. That translated to a 1.47 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, the fourth-worst top season in this class. So why is he even interesting? Because stylistically, he's a bruiser. Law forced 0.35 missed tackles per reception - the best rate among Combine invites. Nearly half of his targets (49.5%) came behind the line of scrimmage, per PFF, the second-highest rate in the class. Roughly 44% of his career receiving yards came on screens, and his average depth of target was the lowest in this year's group. This was a manufactured-touch profile through and through. There's a world where an NFL team leans into that and deploys Law as a gadget weapon. And that kind of role can do something in real football. Fantasy is another story. Since 2020, the only ZAP Model receivers with 25% or more of their yards coming from screens are Lynn Bowden, Anthony Schwartz, Derius Davis, and Malachi Corley. Remember, Law was at nearly 50%. That's not exactly a list of league-winners. Still, there are a few of these quirky, gadget-leaning profiles in this class, and Law could realistically become the best value of that bunch. If he lands in a creative offense and earns early manufactured touches, there's at least some theoretical upside at a cheap rookie draft cost. Just understand what you're buying. This is a scheme-dependent bet.

Evan Engram is a free agent in 2027, and while Joly projects as a good pass-catcher, he was only a fifth-rounder. Bentley probably can find some snaps but is unlikely to garner big receiving volume. LLaattee--RRoouunndd FFaannttaassyy FFoooottbbaallll:: 22002266 PPrroossppeecctt GGuuiiddee JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 113311 The Year 2 Model Overview There's a video game called Rocket League that's been around for a minute, and it's still wildly popular. At its core, it's simple: car soccer. You control a car and try to smash a ball into your opponent's goal. I've played it on and off for years. It's one of those games you can jump into for 20 or 30 minutes, knock out a few five-minute matches, and move on. When you queue up for an online game, Rocket League pairs you with teammates and opponents who have similar skill ratings. In theory, everyone on the field should be roughly equal. In practice? Not always. You can play with friends, but if you queue solo for a two-on-two or three-on-three match, you're paired with random teammates. And those players can be total wildcards. Within the first minute or two, you usually figure it out. Maybe your teammate is clearly outmatched. Maybe they're excellent, but their rating is suppressed by bad luck or poor teammates in previous games. Your expectations adjust quickly once you see real information. The same thing happens in fantasy football. Prospects enter the league with a set of expectations based on what we knew about them coming out of college. Then they play actual NFL games. Suddenly, we have new data that either reinforces or challenges those original assumptions. The question is: how much should those expectations change? That's where the Year 2 Model comes in. The goal of the Year 2 Model is to do a better job predicting a player's future performance - specifically his Year 2 through Year 4 B2S - than simply leaning on his rookie-season points per game. At both running back and wide receiver, the Year 2 Model combines a player's prospect score, certain rookie-season production inputs, and his rookie-year fantasy points per game to generate a Year 2 score. That score is designed to recalibrate expectations using both pre-draft information and what we actually saw on the field. In previous iterations, the model focused on a player's best season in either Year 2 or Year 3. This year, that window has been expanded through Year 4. Why? Because it gives us a larger, more stable sample to work with. Expanding the window allows the model to better capture delayed breakouts, injury interruptions, and uneven early-career development - all things that can distort shorter evaluations. As you'd expect, the Year 2 Model does a much better job predicting a player's next three seasons than rookie-year points per game alone. Just like the ZAP Model, the Year 2 Model can be summarized through probability tables. Players are grouped into the same categories you've already seen - Legendary Performer, Elite Producer, and so on - allowing us to clearly see how often different tiers of players go on to hit specific B2S thresholds.

Kenyon Sadiq.lateround_profile: Kenyon Sadiq became just the seventh tight end drafted 16th or earlier in the NFL Draft since 2011, placing him in rarefied air. The others: Eric Ebron, TJ Hockenson, Kyle Pitts, Brock Bowers, Tyler Warren, and Colston Loveland. Each logged at least one season with 9.3 PPR points per game or more within their first three NFL seasons - a 90th- percentile max early-career season in the database at a minimum. Warren and Loveland have played just one season, too. This group also owns a 54% rate of top-12 seasons in that early-career window while averaging an 18.9% target share. Sadiq's comps look underwhelming, though, so it's worth revisiting how productive Fant, Howard, and Engram actually were early in their careers - B2S scores of 10.2, 9.6, and 12.6, respectively, all 94th-percentile or better. Even Kyle Pitts, perhaps the most obvious "legendary" recent prospect, logged a 93rd-percentile B2S of 9.6 despite a slow start. You can cherry-pick nominal legends like George Kittle (15.6), Mark Andrews (13.2), and Travis Kelce (11.6), but none were even top-60 NFL Draft picks - they simply hit bigger than most tight ends do. Sadiq is a better prospect than any of them. The truth is that there are only a handful of difference-making tight ends every decade, and Sadiq has the building blocks to be one. Sadiq played three seasons at Oregon, running behind significant NFL-caliber talent before breaking out in Year 3 with 51 receptions, a school record at the position. He then broke the tight end record in the 40-yard dash (4.39 seconds) at the Combine - and was drafted just after turning 21. Among first-round tight ends drafted before age 22 in the ZAP Model, even the weakest max early-career season (David Njoku, 29th overall pick) ranked in the 87th percentile. The landing spot with the Jets isn't ideal for reaching a huge offensive ceiling, and for anyone concerned about the production profile for Sadiq, it's a justifiable out. Frank Reich, the team's new coordinator, has historically been friendly to the position, and across 10 seasons where he was a team's coordinator or head coach, his squads have just once ranked outside the top 20 in tight end target share, while ranking in the top five four times. The draft capital, combined with his age-adjusted profile and athleticism, gives Sadiq one of the best ZAP scores the position has ever seen. The model isn't projecting peak Kittle performance in Year 1. It's telling us that Sadiq certainly belongs in the high-end tier of tight end prospects in recent years. Germie Bernard.lateround_profile: Pittsburgh has needed a reliable slot option, and they got one in Alabama's Germie Bernard. Honestly, it's one of the most Steelers picks imaginable. Bernard is tough. He's a solid 206 pounds and forced missed tackles at an 84th percentile rate across his collegiate career. Coaches moved him around, too - he logged 36 rush attempts for 184 yards, including an 18-carry, 101-yard, 2-touchdown performance in 2025 at Alabama. Bernard dealt with strong competition at every stop of his career, which probably had some impact on his overall production. In 2022 at Michigan State, he played alongside Keon Coleman and Jayden Reed. In 2023 at Washington, it was Rome Odunze, Ja'Lynn Polk, Jalen McMillan, and Denzel Boston - and Bernard actually outproduced Boston in that offense. Over the last two years at Alabama, he shared the field with Ryan Williams, a probable first-rounder in 2027. Despite all that, Bernard still posted a 40.4 Breakout Score, which lands in the 52nd percentile. It's not ideal for a second-round pick, but given the context, it's at least understandable. The bull case is straightforward here: Bernard can step in as a go-to slot option and fill a needed role alongside DK Metcalf and Michael Pittman. And he may have a little bit of juice to play outside - his efficiency metrics were better on the perimeter throughout his career than in the slot. The bear case is unfortunately a little easier to make. Bernard finished with a career yards per route run below 2.0, and 22% of his targets came behind the line of scrimmage. Since 2018, the list of top-100 wide receivers with both a sub-2.0 yards per route run and a behind-the-line target rate above 20% includes Jalen Hurd, Lynn Bowden, Amari Rodgers, Velus Jones, and Kyle Williams. If you loosen the yards per route run threshold to 2.2, you get more viable names - Christian Kirk and Zay Flowers, for example - but Bernard's 1.90 career mark still raises concern. Then there's the comp list. It's light on hits, even beyond the three that are provided above. That's not everything, but it's also not something that boosts confidence. Overall, Bernard is fine. The versatility matters, but this looks like more of a higher- floor, lower-ceiling player for fantasy. Pittsburgh does have potential outs with both Metcalf and Pittman's contracts next season, though, which could make things more interesting in 2027. Nate Boerkircher.lateround_profile: There were going to be some surprises at tight end this year in the draft. It was just bound to happen. Nate Boerkircher being selected as the third tight end off the board qualifies as one. Not only that, but it's not like the position as a whole slid down the board. No, the Jaguars drafted him 56th overall. And, to be honest, the ZAP Model hasn't handled many profiles like his before. Boerkircher is already 24 years old and will be 25 by the time the 2026 season starts. Only two other tight ends in the ZAP Model were older than 24 and also a top-64 pick: Luke Schoonmaker and Hayden Hurst (more on them in a second). Boerkircher is now just one of six tight ends in the model with top-64 draft capital despite a Breakout Score under 30 (his is way below that at 13.2). The other five are Dwayne Allen, Hurst, Drew Sample, Schoonmaker, and Brenton Strange (coincidentally, now his Jaguars teammate). There's been sporadic fantasy relevance from those guys, but it's been short-lived and capped early in their careers. However, Boerkircher didn't even test as well athletically as most of those guys did, and that matters. The model allows prospects some leeway with poor production if it's combined with plus athleticism. Boerkircher, though, logged a 4.78-second 40-yard dash - not even at the NFL Combine officially but at his pro day - so we're looking at a 93.2 height- adjusted Speed Score for Boerkircher. Only Dwayne Allen from the aforementioned list also had a height-adjusted Speed Score below 103. Boerkircher is a very specific case in an outlier sense. Yes, he's a second-round pick, but he lacks the athleticism and age-adjusted production that we need to see to buy in. And, yes, Boerkircher will likely be earning snaps with investment this high. Additionally, the Jaguars' tight end room will be wide open starting in 2027 (Brenton Strange, Quintin Morris, and Hunter Long are all free agents next year). Being on the field is step one to fantasy football production, so he has that angle going for him. With that being said, though, his profile is not the type that has turned into consistent fantasy football production in the past. John Michael Gyllenborg.lateround_profile: WYOMING DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk John Michael Gyllenborg had some top-tier athletic testing, but we'll circle back to that - because his profile probably needs some context first. Chris Brazzell.lateround_profile: Chris Brazzell slipped a bit on draft weekend, landing with Carolina at Pick 83. As a second-round prospect, there were questions. As a late Day 2 pick? The upside becomes easier to chase. Brazzell played in Tennessee's offense under Josh Heupel, which hasn't always translated cleanly to the ZAP Model. It's burned us before, so there's some inherent risk here. At 6'4", 198 pounds, Brazzell has the prototypical vertical build. He used it to stretch defenses, too. Among receivers in this class, he saw deep targets at a top-15 rate, and nearly 18% of his receptions came on those throws - a top-10 figure. His career average depth of target, per PFF, ranked top-five. The usage aligns with that role. Brazzell lined up in the slot on fewer than 7% of his snaps, the lowest rate in the class. He's a true boundary receiver tasked with winning downfield. From a ZAP Model standpoint, the profile is solid, but not overwhelming. After starting at Tulane, Brazzell transferred to Tennessee in 2024, with his breakout coming in his final season - the first time he cleared a 2.0 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. That led to a 45.7 Breakout Score, good for the 63rd percentile. We've seen this archetype hit before. Bigger-bodied vertical receivers taken on Day 2 - players like Nico Collins, Tee Higgins, George Pickens, and Christian Watson - show the ceiling that exists here. The issue is that most of those hits had stronger efficiency profiles than Brazzell's 2.09 career yards per route run. Once you get into that range, the comps start to look more like Denzel Mims or Cedric Tillman. Then you factor in the landing spot. Carolina isn't built to support multiple fantasy- relevant wide receivers right now - they ranked 20th in wide receiver PPR production last season. Tetairoa McMillan should command targets, and Jalen Coker looks like a legitimate volume earner as well. This move feels more like a real-life fit than a fantasy-friendly one. Brazzell can help stretch the field and open things up underneath for McMillan and Coker - a role Carolina didn't really have. That could matter for the offense, but it may limit his immediate fantasy ceiling, even if there's maybe some longer-term upside. Kevin Coleman.lateround_profile: Given their depth chart, the Dolphins needed to add at wide receiver in this draft. They took two on Day 2 and then grabbed Kevin Coleman, a wideout who played for four different schools throughout his collegiate career, in Round 5. Coleman is a true slot receiver. Over 91% of his career routes came from the slot - the highest rate in this class. And it wasn't scheme-driven. Across four different programs, he posted slot rates of at least 87% at every stop, per PFF. His best season came at Mississippi State. As a junior, he posted a 2.53 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, which jumped to 2.86 after adjusting for strength of schedule. That led to a 48.2 Breakout Score, seventh-best in the class. The heavy slot usage is the concern. Since 2020, receivers entering the league with a slot rate of at least 90% include KJ Hamler, KJ Hill, Dazz Newsome, Jaelon Darden, Kyle Philips, Derius Davis, Tre Tucker, Malachi Corley, and Jimmy Horn. Like Coleman, that group generally lacked strong draft capital - and it shows in the results. Coleman doesn't overlap much with Miami's earlier picks. Caleb Douglas and Chris Bell project more as perimeter options, while Coleman likely fills a dedicated slot role. That probably gives him the safest projection of the trio, even if Bell carries the higher ceiling. It comes down to preference. In the later rounds of rookie drafts, are you chasing safety or upside? In deeper formats, Coleman makes more sense as a dart. In standard dynasty leagues, he's not a priority target.
