Jaren Kanak.lateround_profile: Jaren Kanak has a really noteworthy profile. It wasn't leading to much in the way of projected draft capital, but he did get selected. The Titans snagged Kanak in the seventh round, 225th overall. Kanak, a high school track athlete and dual-threat quarterback, was a four-star recruit as an athlete with interest from a lot of big programs. He ran a 4.52-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine, equating to a height-adjusted Speed Score of 109.2. While that's not bad by any means, virtually every tight end who ran this year graded out well by that metric. Still, the athleticism alone is noteworthy. However, he measured in at the NFL Combine at 6'2", 234 pounds, making him both the shortest and lightest of all 27 invited tight ends. Noteworthy, though. But get this: Kanak didn't have a catch at Oklahoma until his final season...because he was a linebacker before converting to tight end ahead of the 2025 season. Noteworthy. So, if he got a Combine invite, he must have been relevant this year, right? Yep. He went out and caught 44 passes for 533 yards, both third-best on the Sooners, while playing alongside Combine-invited wide receiver Deion Burks. He wound up being significantly productive when actually playing offense, so his age- and schedule-adjusted max yards per team attempt and Breakout Score are actually above average because he's still relatively young (just 22.2 years old at the time of the draft). Transparently, Kanak has an uphill battle to fantasy football production given his size and the seventh-round status, but we've seen plenty of fantasy-relevant tight ends enter the league with minimal reps at the position - guys who came over from basketball, for example. It's the type of profile that, with more buy-in, we could get more excited about. And, look, tight end isn't a strength for the Titans. Gunnar Helm, a fourth-rounder last year, is set to be the TE1 for them after a promising rookie campaign, and Daniel Bellinger is inked through 2028 after signing with the team this offseason. Kanak could get some schemed touches. He's just unlikely to morph into a high-snap, high-touch player at his size. KC Concepcion.lateround_profile: The fantasy football world probably won't love KC Concepcion landing in Cleveland, but there were definitely worse spots. The Browns have a beatable depth chart, even with Denzel Boston, so Concepcion could realistically lead the team in target share as early as this season. And new head coach Todd Monken is coming off a three-year run as Baltimore's offensive coordinator, where he helped Zay Flowers produce multiple fantasy-relevant campaigns. Flowers, like Concepcion, was a versatile playmaker in college who didn't fit the traditional NFL wide receiver mold. And make no mistake: the ZAP Model likes Concepcion more as a prospect than it liked Flowers. Concepcion is an early declare with a 54.3 Breakout Score (78th percentile), while Flowers posted a lower mark as a four-year player. For a first-round wideout, though, Concepcion's Breakout Score sits right on the line between hit and miss. Among back-half-of-the-first-round receivers in ZAP Model history, nine have posted a score between 45 and 65. That group includes AJ Jenkins, Nelson Agholor, Phillip Dorsett, Laquon Treadwell, and Jalen Reagor - but also Calvin Ridley, Brandon Aiyuk, Zay Flowers, and Brian Thomas. That range alone doesn't give us a clear answer. Other factors help. One is age - as an early declare, Concepcion still isn't 22 years old. Another is versatility, specifically his rushing usage, which feeds into the model. Among 10 wideouts since 2011 in Concepcion's age bucket who were drafted between Picks 16 and 50 and saw their ZAP scores boosted by rushing production, all but two have produced at least one 10-PPR-point-per-game season. The exceptions are Jalen Reagor and Luther Burden, and Burden's only played one NFL season so far. Three players in that cohort - Brandin Cooks, DJ Moore, and Jordan Addison - delivered fringe WR1 seasons, with Robert Woods and Wan'Dale Robinson also in the mix. There are real concerns, though. Concepcion never posted a dominant peak in college - his best season in receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 2.25. Prior to this year's draft, first-round wideouts in the ZAP Model database who failed to hit 2.50 include Henry Ruggs, Kadarius Toney, Zay Flowers, Ricky Pearsall, and Matthew Golden. Concepcion also ran over 70% of his routes from the slot in college, but I'd be less concerned with that given his 2025 campaign with Texas A&M saw him line up in the slot at a 34% rate. And that's when we saw him have his most productive season. This isn't a flawless profile. There are signals pointing in both directions. But the combination of versatility and age-adjusted production gives him a legitimate path to becoming a meaningful NFL and fantasy contributor. Even in Cleveland. Kaytron Allen.lateround_profile: When you first look at Kaytron Allen's production profile, the lack of receiving output stands out immediately. His best-season prorated reception share was just 8.6%, sixth-worst in the class, and his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was 0.50 - also sixth-worst. Allen spent all four seasons at Penn State alongside Nick Singleton, who was drafted just before him. Singleton handled more of the passing-game work, which naturally suppresses Allen's receiving résumé in the model. Stylistically, Allen was the thunder to Singleton's lightning - a downhill, physical runner who forced missed tackles at a slightly higher rate. That said, his tackle-breaking and yards-after-contact numbers were more average than standout relative to this class. When you combine that type of rushing profile with limited receiving involvement, it can be concerning. Historically, it often is. The ZAP Model database includes more than 170 running backs who entered the league with a best-season prorated reception share below 10% and a peak adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt under 0.60 - Allen fits both thresholds. Of that group, just seven produced a season of at least 14 PPR points per game within their first three years: Alfred Morris, Andre Ellington, CJ Anderson, Carlos Hyde, Jordan Howard, Chris Carson, and Isiah Pacheco. This is where Allen's overall production matters. He was highly productive on the ground - he's Penn State's all-time leading rusher - and posted a 1.96 adjusted total yards per team play mark. Among those seven hits, only Jordan Howard and Carlos Hyde reached that level. Add in the fact that Allen shared a backfield with legitimate competition throughout his career, and the profile looks a bit better than the receiving numbers alone suggest. The landing spot in Washington helps, too. There's no entrenched backfield leader, and we know ambiguous situations can create early opportunity for Day 3 picks. Allen is the type of player who could see a short-term spike in dynasty value. If that happens, he may be more of a sell candidate than a long-term hold after Year 1, given the lack of receiving upside - similar to what we've seen with players like Dameon Pierce or even Jordan Howard. But you can still take him in rookie drafts and hope his early-down work wins out in 2026. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 93

Roman Hemby.lateround_profile: INDIANA DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk The advanced numbers don't paint Roman Hemby as a dynamic runner. His career avoided tackles per rush rate is tied for the third-worst in this class. He gained 15 or more yards at the second-lowest rate. His yards after contact per attempt also sits in the bottom half among Combine invites. Those are rushing indicators. And they're not flattering. The ZAP Model is a bit more forgiving because it leans heavily on receiving inputs. At Maryland, Hemby posted an 11% reception share or better in three of his four seasons - the three in which he played more than two games. His adjusted total yards per team play and adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt were below average, but not alarmingly so. Hemby then transferred to Indiana for his final season, where he set a career high with 230 carries. Once you account for offensive environment, though, his attempt share wasn't meaningfully different from what it had been at Maryland. He never exceeded a 40% rush attempt share in college. Put it all together and the profile becomes fairly clear. Hemby projects as a committee back, likely with some pass-catching utility. That's exactly what his statistical comparables suggest: a complementary piece who can contribute, but probably not anchor a backfield on his own.

Caullin Lacy.lateround_profile: LOUISVILLE DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Caullin Lacy measured in at 5'9", 183 pounds at the Combine and ran a 4.55 40. That translated to a 14th percentile Speed Score. In a wide receiver class loaded with high-end testers, that kind of number is going to hurt him on NFL boards. The saving grace is that, for someone this low in the ZAP Model, the production profile isn't a total disaster. It's just not especially inspiring, either. Lacy will turn 25 in December, making him one of the older prospects in the class. That's the byproduct of six collegiate seasons - four at South Alabama and two at Louisville. Not ideal. To Lacy's credit, he did post a legitimate ceiling season. In 2023, he recorded a 3.33 receiving yards per team pass attempt rate. Even after strength-of-schedule adjustments, that number remained strong - his adjusted mark ranks sixth-best in the class. The issue is the broader context. At his size, Lacy operated primarily from the slot, with over 74% of his routes coming from that alignment. And despite the volume and role, his career slot yards per route run stayed under 2.0. That's not the type of efficiency you'd like to see from a smaller, slot-heavy receiver who isn't bringing plus speed to the table. When you combine the age, average athleticism, and middling efficiency, you're left with a fairly low-ceiling projection for fantasy. He could stick as depth or as a role player if things break right. But from a rookie draft perspective, Lacy's not someone you need to prioritize.

Kaden Wetjen.lateround_profile: According to NFL Mock Draft Database, the Steelers selected Kaden Wetjen about 100 picks earlier than expected. That's kind of been the theme of this draft. They didn't take Wetjen for traditional wide receiver production - or at least, they shouldn't have. He caught just 23 passes across three seasons at Iowa, and more than 54% of his targets came behind the line of scrimmage. The goal was simple for his coaches in college: get Wetjen the ball in space and let him create. The production metrics are rough. Wetjen owns the second-lowest best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt in the ZAP Model database. His best-season adjusted fantasy points per game also ranks in the bottom two - out of hundreds and hundreds of wide receivers. Unsurprisingly, Wetjen left school without a Breakout Score. This was almost certainly a special teams-driven pick. Wetjen returned 56 kicks and 54 punts at Iowa - after starting his career at Iowa Western Community College - and won the 2024 Jet Award as the nation's top return specialist. To his credit, he reportedly impressed at the Shrine Bowl, and Iowa's offense hasn't exactly been a pass-heavy environment. But historically, when receiving production is this limited, the odds of translating to fantasy relevance are extremely low. Max Klare.lateround_profile: Despite being considered the TE3 on plenty of big boards entering the draft, Max Klare actually wound up as the TE5, going 61st overall to the Rams, who drafted a fun prospect in Terrance Ferguson (46th overall) just last year. Klare, though, is a fantasy football manager's tight end. That is to say: he should be able to handle a lot of receiving volume - if it comes his way. Klare leads this year's tight end class in Breakout Score, thanks to some big production at Purdue (specifically a 51-catch, 685-yard, 4-touchdown stat line as a redshirt sophomore) before tailing off a bit in his lone year at Ohio State. That's understandable when you're playing alongside Jeremiah Smith, Carnell Tate, and Will Kacmarek (a fellow tight end who was drafted 87th overall this year). So, he carved out great market shares in an easier situation and remained relevant with competition upgrades at Ohio State (he was third on the team in catches behind Smith and Tate this past season). That's good stuff. Playing alongside target-earners like that will also prove to be good practice, as Klare is joining a team with Puka Nacua, Davante Adams, and a crowded tight end room (for now). Klare has some versatility. He was also used out wide fairly often (13.4% wide rate, per PFF, in 2025 - highest among tight ends in the class). While he's unlikely to be the highest-ceiling option we've seen in recent years (especially within this offense, where elite target shares are spoken for already), he should be able to answer the call when the ball is thrown his way. Also, Colby Parkinson and Davis Allen are free agents after this year, so wheels should be up for Klare soon enough, even if he shares the field with Ferguson a lot in the future. Remember, the Rams shifted quite a bit to heavier tight end sets in 2025. Klare almost drew a Mark Andrews comp - just for a little more context. Mike Washington.lateround_profile: Mike Washington was the biggest faller at running back in this class. According to NFL Mock Draft Database, he was expected to go around Pick 63. Instead, he landed in Las Vegas at 122. Using Chase Stuart's draft value chart, that drop equates to a 4.2-point gain for the Raiders. Since 2016, the only players with a similar gap between projected and actual capital are Kenneth Dixon, Dalvin Cook, Paul Perkins, Derrius Guice, and Damien Martinez. The fall isn't all that surprising. Washington is a freak athlete - at 223 pounds, he ran a 4.33 40-yard dash, good for the second-best Speed Score in ZAP Model history. But the production profile is far more ordinary. Washington played five seasons across three programs and never exceeded a 41% rush attempt share. And despite his size, he ranked bottom-five in this class in missed tackles forced per attempt, per PFF. His rate of runs gaining 15 or more yards was also below the class average, even with that elite speed. In other words, the production doesn't really match the measurables. There's also the situation. Washington lands behind Ashton Jeanty, one of the top running back prospects of the last decade. Realistically, that caps him as a handcuff. And there's some value in that role. If Jeanty were to go down, Washington is capable enough to carry the load. It's just easier to buy into a player like this when the profile is stronger. With Washington, it's more of a "fine, I guess" than anything else. If this wasn't within such a mediocre draft class, we'd probably want to be even less bullish. Rahsul Faison.lateround_profile: SOUTH CAROLINA DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Some prospects in this class took winding paths through college. Rahsul Faison went on a full-blown odyssey. First, the headline: Faison is 26 years old. He began his college career in 2019 at Marshall, transferred to Lackawanna College in 2020, then moved to Snow College, where he finally started seeing real playing time. After a couple of seasons there, he transferred to Utah State and spent two years with the Aggies. His best season came there, when he posted a 1.37 total yards per team play rate - easily the top mark of his career. In 2025, Faison transferred again, this time to South Carolina, where he finished with 96 carries and 19 receptions. It was a quiet final chapter. Faison is the oldest player in the ZAP Model database, so we're operating in pretty unusual territory. And age at running back isn't something you can just hand-wave away - he's already brushing up against the typical fantasy age apex before taking an NFL snap. Even beyond the age, the production doesn't do much work. Despite spending six-plus years in college football, Faison never reached a double-digit target share in a season. His best-season adjusted total yards per team play was 1.18, second-worst in this class. There is one encouraging note: at 208 pounds, he forced missed tackles at one of the best rates in the group. Only two backs in this class posted a better career missed tackles forced rate. But let's be real: Faison was a 25-year-old at one point playing against teenagers. That edge doesn't translate cleanly forward. This is the type of profile that's easy to fade in rookie drafts. The age, the lack of a true spike season, and the limited receiving production create a very mid prospect. Jonah Coleman.lateround_profile: Jonah Coleman is a true do-it-all back. He began his collegiate career at Arizona, logging over 200 combined carries across his freshman and sophomore seasons. After transferring to Washington for his final two years, he elevated his profile further. Coleman's peak in total yards per team play came as a junior, though that season included a reception share below 8%. As a senior, he put it all together, posting a similar yards- per-team-play mark while nearly doubling his prorated reception share to 13.2%. Stylistically, he looks like a powerful workhorse. According to PFF, no running back in this class forced missed tackles at a higher rate per attempt. He also ranked top-five in yards after contact per attempt and earned one of the strongest pass-blocking grades among Combine invitees. There are some questions about long speed - and he didn't test athletically - but his career explosive run rate is still above the class average. He may not be a burner, but he's not lacking big-play ability. The landing spot in Denver is especially interesting. The Broncos drafted RJ Harvey in Round 2 last year, but his rookie season was underwhelming. Per Next Gen Stats, Harvey ranked 46th in rushing yards over expected per attempt among 48 qualified running backs, and his success rate ranked 32nd - well behind J.K. Dobbins, who was 12th in the same offense. And Dobbins himself could be gone after the 2026 season. There's a real scenario where Coleman ends up being the best running back in this backfield. That doesn't mean it happens right away - or that it's a certainty - but the combination of his profile and the uncertainty around him makes it possible. If the market discounts that in rookie drafts, it's worth taking advantage. Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2026 Prospect Guide Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model 85

Michael Trigg.lateround_profile: BAYLOR FLEX PLAY Low Risk Neutral High Risk Michael Trigg's profile is that of a well-rounded athlete. He played defensive end and basketball in high school and earned four-star recruiting status before heading to Southern California, the first of three NCAA schools for which he suited up. Trigg played only six games as a Trojan in 2021 and then seven and three games at Ole Miss in 2022 and 2023, respectively. Across those 16 games, he totaled just 28 catches for 330 yards and 5 touchdowns. But then at Baylor in 2024, Trigg notched 30 catches for 395 yards and 3 touchdowns and built on it in 2025: 50 grabs for 694 yards and 6 touchdowns, ranking top-three on the team in each of those categories in his final NCAA season. Even with that slow start, his best season by age- and schedule-adjusted max yards per team attempt is about average among the tight end database, as was his Breakout Score. We do have to note, though, that Trigg missed time not only for injuries but also for off-field incidents, and while it's possible that the production profile could've been markedly better, film and scouting grades put his blocking into question. It could all be interconnected. I'm focused on the data, but this is part of the equation with Trigg, whose draft stock has been declining in the months leading up to the NFL Draft. For us as fantasy football managers, we care a lot about the receiving production, but we also know that poor blocking and commitment can mean reduced snaps. It can also translate into a new-age tight end who is tasked more with receiving than blocking. Trigg's yards per route run data was good at all his stops, including a 2.18 mark in his last season, and he led this year's Combine-invited class in slot rate, per PFF. That's what makes him appealing as a fantasy football prospect. He could be a good-to-great NFL receiver. Trigg's long arms and big hands, as measured at the Combine, help make for a highlight- level catch radius, which he certainly flashed on tape. The big board rank has been falling, but if an NFL team sees it differently and applies some capital in the late- Round-2 or middle-of-Round 3 range where he peaked, he draws some Tucker Kraft and Brenton Strange comps. Trigg could be legit if an NFL team uses him to his best abilities. CJ Donaldson.lateround_profile: OHIO STATE DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk When you weigh 230 pounds and rank dead last in your draft class in career missed tackles forced per attempt, that's a red flag. That's the starting point with CJ Donaldson. Among 220-plus-pound running backs since 2020, the only ones with missed tackles forced rates in his range are Gerrid Doaks and Stevie Scott. Not great! Donaldson spent three seasons at West Virginia before transferring to Ohio State for his final year. He was originally recruited as a tight end, which makes sense when you see the 6'2", 230-pound frame. Across four collegiate seasons, Donaldson never truly controlled his team's rushing workload. His best attempt share was around 31%. Efficiency didn't pick up the slack, either. He posted the third-lowest explosive run rate in this year's class and the second- worst career yards after contact per attempt. The receiving profile offers little relief. His best-season reception share was just 6.2%, better than only Jadarian Price among running backs invited to the NFL Combine. So what's the bet? That an NFL team sees the size and thinks it can mold him into something more physical and efficient than he showed in college. Maybe that happens. But historically, backs with this combination of low tackle-breaking ability, modest workload share, and minimal receiving production don't turn into fantasy staples. If Donaldson carves out a role, it's likely as more of a situational back. That's not the kind of archetype you're drafting with confidence in any fantasy format.

Carnell Tate.lateround_profile: We've seen Chris Olave, Garrett Wilson, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Marvin Harrison Jr., and Emeka Egbuka go in Round 1 out of Ohio State over the last five years. Now we can add Carnell Tate to that list. Tate went fourth overall to Tennessee, and while that draft capital is as strong as any Buckeye receiver in this run, his ZAP score ranks second-lowest among them, ahead of only Egbuka. That's not necessarily a knock - his 65.3 Breakout Score sits in the 87th percentile - it's just that the others brought slightly stronger analytical profiles to the table. The biggest concern with Tate is volume, which obviously matters for fantasy. Yes, he played alongside Jeremiyah Smith, but his best-season target share still maxed out at just 21.7%. Among first-round wide receivers since 2018 (36 entering this year), that number only beats Henry Ruggs, Jaylen Waddle, Kadarius Toney, Jameson Williams, and Matthew Golden. It doesn't stop there. Tate earned a target on fewer than 20% of his routes run across his collegiate career. Among that same first-round cohort since 2018, the only others to hit that low of a targets per route run rate are Ruggs, Williams, Brian Thomas, Xavier Legette, Ricky Pearsall, Golden, and Travis Hunter. It's not all doom and gloom, though. Top-10 draft capital matters - a lot. Since 2011, wide receivers drafted in the top half of Round 1 who played at least three seasons have averaged a best-season output of 13.9 PPR points per game within their first three years. When you filter for players with Breakout Scores above 60, that jumps to 15.0. That gives Tate a strong, high-end median outcome. The landing spot in Tennessee isn't bad, either. Cam Ward is still a bit of a question mark, but the arm talent is there. Tate wins downfield and in contested situations - his collegiate contested catch rate ranked in the 98th percentile among ZAP Model wideouts since 2020. That should mesh well with Ward. And there's a path to volume. The Titans added Wan'Dale Robinson this offseason, but he'll operate more underneath. Elic Ayomanor flashed as a rookie, but Tate can take over the field-stretching role right away. And Calvin Ridley - ironically one of Tate's ZAP comps - looks closer to the end than the peak at this point. All told, a ZAP score of 88.3 is really good - like, really good. He's not quite a Legendary Performer, but this is a profile you can work with, even if there are some analytical concerns. Eric Rivers.lateround_profile: GEORGIA TECH DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Eric Rivers owns the second-best single-season yards per route run mark in this class. Seriously. His career yards per route run sits at 2.40 - eighth-best among Combine invite wide receivers. So... do we have something here? Probably not. Rivers will turn 24 in September, putting him on the older side of the prospect spectrum. He played two years at Memphis with minimal production, transferred to Florida International for two more, and then finished at Georgia Tech. That eye-popping 3.57 yards per route run season came in his second year at FIU. The seasons surrounding it? 1.32 and 2.07. One of those years is not like the others. That outlier campaign was strong enough to earn him a 32.8 Breakout Score, which isn't nothing for a projected Day 3 pick. But it's not particularly impressive either - especially for a 176-pound receiver. We saw a ceiling season, sure. But it came at FIU, not exactly against elite competition. When you zoom out, this looks more like a one-year spike than a sustainably dominant profile.

Caleb Douglas.lateround_profile: Entering the draft, Miami's wide receiver depth chart was arguably the weakest in the league. Taking Caleb Douglas in Round 3 didn't do much to change that. Maybe Douglas proves the ZAP Model wrong, but there's not a lot to like here. He was projected to go outside the top 200, according to NFL Mock Draft Database, and the underlying metrics reflect that. Douglas posted a 25th percentile Breakout Score, a 28th percentile career yards per route run, a 15th percentile best-season yards per route run, a 23rd percentile first downs per route run, a...you get the idea. One of the bigger concerns is where and how he wins. Douglas ran just 15% of his routes from the slot, but his efficiency on the perimeter was especially poor - just 1.65 yards per route run, a 21st percentile mark. At 6'4", 206 pounds, he's almost certainly going to be asked to play outside in the NFL. According to PFF data, Douglas is just the ninth Day 2 wide receiver to enter the league since 2020 with a career perimeter yards per route run below 1.80. None of the previous eight have produced more than 12.5 PPR points per game in any of their first three seasons. He'll get a chance to see the field - that depth chart all but guarantees it. But if you're throwing a dart at a Miami wide receiver from this draft, Chris Bell offers a more appealing ceiling. We'll just have to see where the dynasty market places Douglas to know if he's a clear target or avoid - all we do know is that this is clearly a high-risk profile. Bauer Sharp.lateround_profile: A better-than-projected 40-yard dash time of 4.63 at the NFL Combine raised Bauer Sharp's stock in the pre-draft model, but he was still a long shot and was projected to go undrafted at the time despite a 110 height-adjusted Speed Score. He still is a long shot, of course, but he was a sixth-round pick, 185th overall, by the Buccaneers, who were rumored to be interested in a tight end as early as the first round. That's because their tight end room is pretty barren behind Cade Otton, who is under contract through the 2028 season. Then there are some low-volume names in Ko Kieft and Payne Durham signed through 2026, plus Devin Culp, who is signed through 2027. There's a chance Sharp can break through here. In college, Sharp spent three seasons at Southeastern Louisiana and was a wildcat quarterback for a time before making the move to Oklahoma and then to LSU. To say he just moved to Oklahoma would be a little bit of an understatement: he led the team in receptions and yards in his first year as a Sooner. To get too excited about that would be a bit of an overstatement, though: he had 42 catches for 324 yards (7.7 per reception) with 2 touchdowns. At LSU in 2025, he finished sixth on the team in catches (24) and yards (252) in his fifth and final college season. That's still a strong overall progress curve, and it's a great story. The max yards per team attempt rate is good enough for the 38th percentile; the Breakout Score (24th percentile) is less enticing. Additionally, while he has just two years of charted PFF data, Sharp enters with the lowest average depth of target of the class: under 5.0 yards. It's still poor when you adjust for how his tight end teammates were used. Good athleticism with weak draft capital and production is telling us plenty about how Sharp is able to use that athleticism. It's a core philosophy of the tight end model. As a result, Sharp isn't expected to make much of an impact on Sundays, but it's also not like he's blocked out by tough competition in Tampa Bay, leaving the door open for him to outperform expectations if the tenacity as a blocker carries over and earns him snaps and if the athleticism earns him some schemed targets. Matthew Hibner.lateround_profile: After a big run on tight ends on Day 2, it took a while for the position to be called upon on Day 3. Matthew Hibner was the first tight end taken in Round 4 when he went 133rd overall to the Baltimore Ravens, joining a tight end room that is pretty barren after the team parted ways with Isaiah Likely in the offseason. Mark Andrews is locked up through the 2028 season, so we can't gloss over that, but Durham Smythe's contract is up after 2026. And that's really it. So, there is a pretty immediate opportunity for Hibner to be the TE2 in Baltimore. What makes Hibner the TE10 in the class, though? It's a good question because he was projected to be a seventh-round pick (or undrafted) for most of the lead-in to the draft. Hibner spent six years in college (four at Michigan, including 2020 when he saw no action as a freshman, and two at Southern Methodist). Despite half a dozen collegiate seasons, Hibner failed to exceed 1.0 yards per team attempt in any season after adjusting for age and schedule, and he ultimately lands in the 20th percentile by best-season max yards per team attempt in the tight end database. However, Hibner did have competition at tight end in his career. Kind of a lot of it. The past two seasons at SMU, he shared the field with RJ Maryland, who was also invited to the NFL Combine this year. Before that, he was at Michigan, overlapping with Colston Loveland, AJ Barner, and Marlin Klein (who went 59th this year in the draft). So, no, the production isn't amazing, but the draft capital is solid - and so is the landing spot. Now, add in the athleticism, and we have a better picture of the potential here. Hibner ran a 4.57-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine. That was fifth-best among tight ends who ran and equates to a height-adjusted Speed Score over 115. He's yet another athletic tight end without a complete production profile that an NFL team bought in on during the 2026 NFL Draft, perhaps signaling a sea change for how franchises value tight end prospects in the modern NFL. Athleticism and traits may be winning out over raw receiving production. Hibner is already 24, though, and that's the biggest knock of all. Recalibrated comps for Hibner at better draft capital than expected would paint a picture more like a Luke Willson or perhaps a Chig Okonwko on the optimistic end of things. Demond Claiborne.lateround_profile: You've seen it in other profiles, but for Day 3 running backs to hit, they often need to land in semi-ambiguous backfields. If their teammates aren't being drafted highly in season-long formats, it's a signal that there's no clear starter. And where there's uncertainty, there's opportunity. Demond Claiborne lands in one of those situations in Minnesota. Size could be a limiting factor. At 5'10", 188 pounds, the Wake Forest product handled a solid - but not dominant - workload across four seasons. His peak came as a junior with 228 carries, the only time he cleared 200 attempts. There's some pass-game involvement, with a 13.1% best-season reception share. But the impact was modest. He never exceeded a 0.63 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, and once you factor in age and strength of schedule, that falls below the class average. The straight-line speed is real - he ran a 4.37 40-yard dash at the Combine. The issue is that the big-play profile doesn't fully align with that speed. Per PFF, Claiborne posted a 12.3% explosive run rate, below the class average of 13.8%. Over the last five years, the only back under 200 pounds with an explosive run rate below 15% to even reach 4 PPR points per game in a season is Kyren Williams - and Williams had a much stronger overall production profile. Claiborne also didn't stand out in missed tackles forced or yards after contact per attempt. For a lighter back, you'd ideally want at least one of those traits to pop. This looks more like a complementary back than a true bell-cow - and that's fine. This class isn't loaded with high-end running back talent, and Claiborne's skill set pairs well with Minnesota's current early-down option, Jordan Mason. There's also a longer-term angle. Both Mason and Aaron Jones are set to hit free agency next year, so if Claiborne flashes at all, there's a path to more volume in 2027. Eli Heidenreich.lateround_profile: Chances are, as a seventh-round pick in a pretty throwaway landing spot, Eli Heidenreich won't become anything meaningful for fantasy football. But, man, what a cool story and profile. Heidenreich went to Mt. Lebanon High School, right outside Pittsburgh. (I went to North Allegheny, also right outside Pittsburgh, but we won't get into which high school is better. Now isn't the time.) He then spent his college career at Navy. And, well, service academies don't throw the ball. Since 2011, Army, Navy, and Air Force have combined to reach 300 team pass attempts in a season just twice. So when you're evaluating a player in that kind of environment, the numbers can get... weird. That's exactly what happened with Heidenreich in 2025. Heidenreich was a productive piece of Navy's offense for three seasons, but 2025 was the breakout: 51 receptions and a staggering 48.1% prorated reception share. That's the highest mark in ZAP Model history. By roughly 23 percentage points. He's not a traditional running back. He's more of a Swiss Army knife - a movable chess piece who can align in the backfield, in the slot, or out wide. He carried the ball 77 times last season but was deployed all over the formation as a receiver. Any team drafting him is doing so with that versatility in mind. And the receiving metrics? They're absurd. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt sits at 4.96 - the best mark in the model's database. Second place? Christian McCaffrey at 2.36. Yeah. For real. Like I said at the top, the odds aren't great that Heidenreich becomes anything meaningful for fantasy purposes. These tweener profiles usually need a very specific situation to hit. But this class isn't very good. At all. So I'm going to say something I don't say often as a process-oriented analyst: Why not have a little fun and shoot for the outlier? Ted Hurst.lateround_profile: Ted Hurst gained a lot of steam during the pre-draft process, rising from a projected 180th overall pick in mid-January to a top-80 selection by April. He ultimately went 84th overall - a big win for a small-school prospect. Analytically, Hurst is tough to pin down. His Breakout Score sits around 42, which is basically average, but he's a younger prospect (still under 22) with good length. He was also used aggressively downfield in college. His 28.6% career deep target rate ranks top- 10 in this class, and more than 21% of his receptions came on deep throws - fourth-highest among Combine invites. That usage drove a top-10 average depth of target. That all sounds fine, but we should keep in mind that it came against weaker competition. His best season in adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 2.08. Among 26 Day 2 wide receivers in the ZAP Model database with a mark below 2.2, only 5 have produced a best season above 10 PPR points per game within their first three years. It's not an awful hit rate when you consider context, but it's not encouraging either. Layer in the deep-ball profile, and the risk increases. Since 2020, 22 non-first-round wide receivers have entered the league with at least 20% of their receptions coming on deep targets and a deep target rate above 25%. Only one - Nico Collins - has produced a season above 12 PPR points per game within his first three years. At least Collins shows up as a comp! In a class without many obvious targets, Hurst offers some intrigue. Tampa Bay wouldn't be the most ideal landing spot for fantasy, but there's a path. Mike Evans, their primary perimeter downfield threat, is gone. Chris Godwin could be out next year given his contract. Jalen McMillan has flashed, and Tez Johnson has shown something as well, but Hurst's range of outcomes includes being the best of that trio. There's at least a scenario - and it's not the most likely one - where Hurst is earning targets behind only Emeka Egbuka for the Buccaneers by 2027. Just don't draft him as if that outcome is guaranteed. And certainly don't draft him with the expectation that he'll have a monster 2026 campaign. You're getting him for 2027 and beyond. Robert Henry.lateround_profile: TEXAS-EL PASO DART THROW Low Risk Neutral High Risk Robert Henry's best-season reception share was 12.3%, which is respectable on the surface. The problem is that it came with major inefficiency, largely due to adjustments. In that same season, his age- and schedule-adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 0.40, the fourth-worst mark in this class. Context doesn't really save it, either. Henry played at Jones College and UTEP, and he reached higher than a 7% reception share in just that one campaign. He wasn't facing elite weekly competition - the production simply wasn't there in an impactful way. Athletically, there's not much of a boost. At 196 pounds, Henry ran a 4.52 40 at the Combine, resulting in a below average 93.9 Speed Score. Historically, that's a tough combo. In the ZAP Model database, 111 running backs have posted both a sub-100 Speed Score and a sub-0.5 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate. Only nine of them reached 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons: Alfred Morris, Stevan Ridley, Andre Ellington, Alex Collins, Thomas Rawls, Jordan Howard, Chris Carson, Ronald Jones, and James Robinson. Every single one of those backs weighed more than Henry at the Combine. Add in the age - he'll be 24 after five collegiate seasons - and the odds shrink even further. Older running back prospects haven't produced much sustained fantasy success historically. Even in a favorable landing spot, Henry doesn't project cleanly into a three-down role. He's not built like a classic early-down bruiser, and the receiving profile doesn't suggest a natural passing-down specialist, either. That's an awkward middle ground to live in. And it's not where you want your Day 3 dart throw to sit.

Will Kacmarek.lateround_profile: Will Kacmarek was projected to go undrafted when the prospect guide was first published. The Dolphins made him a third-round pick, 87th overall. This tight end class is something else, man. The class is highlighted primarily by two smaller, pass-catching tight ends in Kenyon Sadiq and Eli Stowers. Kacmarek is certainly not that. He weighed in at 261 pounds at the NFL Combine, making him the second-heaviest tight end in the class (of the 27 players invited). Kacmarek also ran a 4.74-second 40-yard dash at a nice size for a 106.1 height-adjusted Speed Score. So, there's size and athleticism to go along with draft capital. Was there production? Not really, no. Kacmarek played three seasons at Ohio before transferring to Ohio State for two more years. Even at Ohio, he didn't average more than 19.0 yards per game in either season, and he caught just 23 passes in 26 games at Ohio State. In college, Kacmarek was primarily a blocking tight end with some good per-route receiving data (the Dolphins' Instagram post welcoming Kacmarek to the team actually starts by calling him "a run-blocking specialist"). As far as Miami's tight end room is concerned, there's not much in the sense of consistent receiving production, but Kacmarek may not be the answer there. Top-90 draft capital on a big-bodied, plus athlete isn't totally irrelevant. But the high- risk flag here suggests that Kacmarek was a reach in the NFL Draft when viewed from a fantasy football lens. Bryce Lance.lateround_profile: The Saints took Jordyn Tyson in Round 1, followed it up with tight end Oscar Delp in Round 3, and then added Bryce Lance late in Round 4. From a fit standpoint, it makes sense. Lance is a big-bodied perimeter receiver who can stretch the field and complement their existing playmakers - if he develops. That's a big "if." Lance spent five years at North Dakota State, redshirting as a true freshman and mostly playing special teams over the next two seasons. Through 2023, he had just one career reception. At an FCS powerhouse, he didn't see meaningful production until his fourth year on campus - after turning 23. To his credit, the final two seasons were productive. He posted a 2.70 prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt in 2024 and followed it with a massive 4.00 mark in 2025. But once you adjust for competition, that 4.00 drops below 2.50. And it's the only meaningful adjusted season of his career. With such a late breakout, Lance doesn't register a Breakout Score. That's not uncommon. What is uncommon is becoming fantasy relevant without one. Among more than 100 wide receivers in the ZAP Model without a Breakout Score, only Hunter Renfrow, Tyreek Hill, and Terry McLaurin have posted a double-digit PPR points-per-game season within their first three years. Hill is a unicorn. McLaurin had strong draft capital. Renfrow's peak didn't last long. Lance has traits. He's 6'3", 204 pounds and ran a 4.34 40-yard dash at the Combine. But this is a bet on tools over production. And those bets don't hit often, even if the upside is there. I just like this fit more from a real football perspective than a fantasy one. Jack Endries.lateround_profile: Jack Endries has a strong tie to the 2025 Heisman Trophy winner, Fernando Mendoza. They played two seasons together in 2023 and 2024 at Cal before transferring to different schools. Endries, as a redshirt sophomore, led the Golden Bears in receptions (56) and yards (623). But their draft capital couldn't have been much different. Mendoza, of course, went first overall to the Raiders. Endries was selected 221st overall, in the seventh round, by the Bengals. He does possess solid age-adjusted production and an athletic baseline, however. Endries, in total, rates out with strong max yards per team attempt data (67th percentile) and good Breakout Score (73rd percentile). He also ran a 4.62-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine, giving him a height-adjusted Speed Score of 109 at 6'5", 245. On the flip side, despite a smaller frame and good athletic testing, Endries doesn't pop from a yards-after-the-catch standpoint. That's also tied to some average-to-below-average blocking scores from PFF and SportsInfoSolutions (and it's worth noting that he graded out with around 15th-percentile arm length). Endries had a pretty wide range of pre-draft valuations, spanning from the middle of the second round to one of the first names being called on Day 3. He wound up being one of the last names called on Day 3. The Bengals, though, have just two tight ends signed through 2027 (the 31-year-old Mike Gesicki and the 26-year-old Erick All). Endries had everything but draft capital in his profile, and at a certain point in the draft, athleticism isn't usually what wins out. It's a history of producing. That's at least some hope. The odds he hits big are incredibly low, but if he begins to find footing in a high-octane offense like Cincinnati's, well, maybe he plays more like a Day 2 pick that some thought he could be before the draft.
